title: - _Never Train from Scratch_ : FAIR COMPARISON OF LONG - SEQUENCE MODELS REQUIRES DATA DRIVEN PRIORS [PDF]

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024

## - _Never Train from Scratch_ : FAIR COMPARISON OF LONG - SEQUENCE MODELS REQUIRES DATA DRIVEN PRIORS

**Ido Amos Jonathan Berant Ankit Gupta** Tel Aviv University _[∗]_ Tel Aviv University IBM Research

## ABSTRACT

Modeling long-range dependencies across sequences is a longstanding goal in machine learning and has led to architectures, such as state space models, that dramatically outperform Transformers on long sequences. However, these impressive empirical gains have been by and large demonstrated on benchmarks (e.g. Long Range Arena), where models are randomly initialized and trained to predict a target label from an input sequence. In this work, we show that random initialization leads to gross overestimation of the differences between architectures and that pretraining with standard denoising objectives, using _only the downstream task data_ , leads to dramatic gains across multiple architectures and to very small gaps between Transformers and state space models (SSMs). In stark contrast to prior works, we find vanilla Transformers to match the performance of S4 on Long Range Arena when properly pretrained, and we improve the best reported results of SSMs on the PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points. Subsequently, we analyze the utility of previously-proposed structured parameterizations for SSMs and show they become mostly redundant in the presence of data-driven initialization obtained through pretraining. Our work shows that, when evaluating different architectures on supervised tasks, incorporation of data-driven priors via pretraining is essential for reliable performance estimation, and can be done efficiently.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Self-supervised pretraining is now widespread across most areas of machine learning, including NLP, speech, and vision (Touvron et al., 2023; Baevski et al., 2020; Reed et al., 2022). Given a downstream task, it is standard to finetune a pretrained model rather than train “from scratch”, to achieve better performance (Raffel et al., 2019). Conversely, when developing new architectures with better inductive biases for particular skills, for example, for capturing long-range dependencies or for better algorithmic reasoning, it is still common to train on the task data from scratch with random initialization (Tay et al., 2020a; Deletang et al., 2022; Velivckovi’c et al., 2022; Dwivedi´ et al., 2022). This difference in practice stems not only from the computational overhead required for pretraining on massive datasets, but also to decouple the effects of the pretraining data and allow an apples-to-apples comparison, which would otherwise require a “standard” pretraining corpus for each scenario.

A prime example of the latter scenario is estimating capabilities in modeling long range dependencies in sequences, a setting where Transformers have reported inadequate performance on benchmarks designed as stress tests, such as Long Range Arena (LRA) (Tay et al., 2020a). This inefficacy of Transformers has led to a line of new architectures, suggesting changes to RNNs, CNNs and Transformers themselves, biasing them towards capturing long range dependencies, and achieving impressive performance on LRA, when trained from scratch (Gu et al., 2022a; Gupta et al., 2022a; Li et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2022). However, these results do not align with performance of pretrained Transformers (“foundation models”), that have displayed remarkable performance on tasks involving modeling long range dependencies, such as text summarization, code completion and protein folding, (Touvron et al., 2023; Jumper et al., 2021). Despite the significant progress in long sequence modeling, the reasons for sub-par performance of Transformers on long sequence benchmarks, such as LRA,

remains unexplored, while methods achieving competitive performance resort to tailored changes to the architecture (Ma et al., 2022; Zuo et al., 2022).

In this work, we shed light on this discrepancy, showing it stems from inadequate training and evaluation practices, and suggest a simple and efficient solution allowing a proper evaluation. While avoiding pretraining on a large corpus is understandable, training from a random initialization with downstream supervision alone disregards the role of the pretraining objective itself, leading to a different inductive bias than that of a pretrained model. In a recent line of work, El-Nouby et al. (2021); He et al. (2022); Krishna et al. (2023) have demonstrated that, when using denoising objectives, pretraining solely on downstream training data (denoted as _self pretraining_ ) often leads to gains comparable to the ones from pretraining on large corpora, showing effectiveness on tasks such as image classification, segmentation, text classification, etc. This suggests that, rather then training from scratch, a more realistic estimate of model performance can be obtained via self pretraining (SPT), with SPT acting as a data-driven initialization method, while allowing a fair comparison between methods as only the task data is used.

To demonstrate the importance of the suggested method, we empirically show that priors learned through SPT with denoising objectives are highly effective for learning long range dependencies across several architectures, eliminating the need for complex hand-crafted modeling biases used in current solutions (Gu et al., 2022a; Ma et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022; Orvieto et al., 2023). We primarily study Long Range Arena (LRA), a standard benchmark for long sequence modeling and evaluate multiple SPT models. We show that SPT improves the mean absolute performance of vanilla Transformers by more than 30%, for the first time allowing them to match the state-of-the-art performance on LRA without any architectural changes (Figure 1). This is in stark contrast to prior works where Transformers report significantly lower performance compared to the state-of-the-art.

We study the effectiveness of SPT for State Space models (SSMs), a novel line of architectures using modified linear RNNs as a replacement for attention layers in Transformers. Incorporating a specialized parameterization and initialization of linear RNNs, SSMs such as S4 successfully mitigate the vanishing/exploding gradient issues and reach impressive performance on long sequence tasks, such as LRA (Gu et al., 2022a). We find SPT to also benefit S4 with performance gains in 5 out of 6 LRA tasks. Moreover, with SPT, S4 solves the challenging PathX-256 task, achieving a 20% accuracy improvement compared to training from scratch (Figure 1). Building on these improvements, we study the utility of hand-crafted modeling biases in S4 over simpler linear RNNs, finding that the data-driven priors learned via SPT render most of them redundant (Gupta et al., 2022b). In doing so, we are the first to provide competitive performance with diagonal linear RNNs without any manual modifications (Orvieto et al., 2023).

Our findings show that priors beneficial for capturing distant dependencies can be simply learned from the task data via standard denoising objectives without any intrusive changes to the model. We examine the benefits of SPT across multiple data scales showing them to become even more pronounced as data becomes relatively scarce. Last, for SSMs, we analyze the convolution kernels

learned via SPT to shed light on the learned priors for capturing long-range dependencies. We demonstrate an interesting phenomena in which, depending on the modality, rapidly decaying kernels can lead to improved performance over the slowly decaying ones as used in the native S4 model, further highlighting the utility of learning priors from the data itself (Gu et al., 2020).

Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:

- _(i)_ We show that the reported performance of various architectures on long range benchmarks is grossly underestimated, and suggest an inexpensive data-driven approach to enable accurate evaluation without requiring any additional data.

- _(ii)_ We report large empirical gains over the previously-reported performances on LRA across a range of architectures and, in particular, improve upon the best reported accuracy on the challenging PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points (67 _→_ 87).

- _(iii)_ We demonstrate how manually-designed biases become increasingly redundant with pretraining and that, with modern training and evaluation practices, simpler models can often match the performance of sophisticated architectures. We are the first to provide competitive performance on LRA with Transformers and diagonal linear RNNs.

The multi-modal and challenging setup of LRA, along with the scale of improvements due to SPT, advocate the inclusion of a pretraining stage while evaluating models in general, for example when designing architectures for multidimensional inputs (Nguyen et al., 2022), algorithmic reasoning (Diao & Loynd, 2023) or graphs (Shirzad et al., 2023).

Our code & data are available at https://github.com/IdoAmos/not-from-scratch.

## 2 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

Our experiments center around the evaluation of Transformers and SSMs on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark which was proposed for examining the ability of sequence models to capture long-range dependencies (Tay et al., 2020a). It contains 6 main sequence classification tasks, each being either binary or 10-way sequence classification.

1. ListOps: Each sequence in the dataset is a nested list, with each sublist describing an operation (e.g. MAX, MEAN) to be applied on a set of tokens (Nangia & Bowman, 2018). Evaluation of nested lists are used as a single token in their enclosing list thus requiring the understanding of hierarchical structure, the task is 10-way classification with sequence length of 2 _K_ .

## **INPUT:** [MAX 4 3[MIN 2 3]1 0[MEDIAN 1 5 8 9 2]] **OUTPUT:** 5

2. Text: a character-level version of the IMDb reviews dataset (Maas et al., 2011) for sentiment classification, the task is binary classification with sequence length of up to 2048.

3. Retrieval: a character-level version of the AAN dataset (Radev et al., 2013) for predicting similarity scores of two documents. The task is binary classification with sequence length of up to 4 _K_ , requiring to process 8 _K_ tokens for evaluation.
4. Image: grayscale CIFAR10 images are flattened as 1D sequences and any explicit 2D inductive bias cannot be used. The task is 10-way classification, with sequence length 1024.

5. Pathfinder, PathX: synthetic 2D visual tasks treated as 1D sequences (similar to Image) for testing tracing capabilities (Linsley et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2020). PathX and Pathfinder are similar tasks that differ in sequence length (1024 vs 16384) and are binary classification.

Apart from the aforementioned tasks, we examine an additional variant of PathX called PathX-256 with sequence length 256[2] = 65536 and we are the first to report strong results on this task. Besides LRA, we experiment with additional datasets that will be described later in Section 3.7.

**Self Pretraining (SPT)** We perform SPT with a causal/autoregressive sequence modeling objective for unidirectional models, and a masked sequence modeling objective for bidirectional models, using _only_ the downstream task training set. For the visual tasks (Image, Pathfinder, Path-X) the masking ratio for masked sequence modeling is set to 50% following He et al. (2022), to 15% for language

## 3 RESULTS

In Section 3.1, we perform SPT for LRA tasks using the official model configurations. In Section 3.2, we perform SPT for Transformers and S4. Section 3.3 evaluates the role of design choices in SSMs in the context of SPT. Section 3.4 examines the utility of SPT across data scales and Section 3.5 examines the utility of PT on a large text corpus. Section 3.6 provides an analysis of pretrained SSM kernels and how they relate to current initialization schemes. Section 3.7 contains additional experiments on distinct modalities.

## 3.1 UNDERESTIMATION OF LONG-RANGE ABILITIES OF TRANSFORMERS

We start by investigating the reliability of the historically-reported model performances on LRA, in the more modern setting of pretraining. Concretely, we repeat the Transformer experiments performed by Tay et al. (2020a), except that we first pretrain the model on the task data and then finetune it. To allow fair comparison with the original results, we strictly follow the model configurations used by Tay et al. (2020a). We experiment with two pretraining objectives: (1) next token prediction for unidirectional models (2) masked token prediction for bidirectional models, varying the masking ratio as detailed in Section 2.

As summarized in Table 1, we find that both pretraining objectives lead to dramatic performance gains for Transformers compared to the conventional practice of training with random initialization, with the average test accuracy increasing by roughly 30%. Both causal and masked pretraining yield similar results even in cases where there are no clear benefits to using a causal model, such as on the visual tasks. Furthermore, even for LISTOPS large performance gains are observed even though, in the original data, the arguments to the list operations are sampled randomly, meaning that inferring missing tokens from the context is rarely possible.

As the experiments are performed with no architectural changes or additional data, the difference in performances can be attributed to the priors learned during SPT, clearly demonstrating its importance for a reliable performance evaluation.

## 3.2 COMPARING S4 AND TRANSFORMERS

In the above set-up we strictly adhered to the model sizes used by Tay et al. (2020a) and consequently the absolute performances are still low compared to the current state-of-the-art on LRA. In this section, we scale the model sizes and evaluate the utility of SPT for the best performing architectures including S4 (Gu et al., 2022a). For Transformers, we replace the positional embeddings with the more commonly used rotary embeddings (Su et al., 2021) and only train bidirectional models in line with prior works reporting high performance.

As summarized in Table 2, SPT leads to dramatic performance gains for Transformers with performance gains ranging from 8 _−_ 15% across tasks, even surpassing the average performance of a well-tuned S4 (86 _._ 2 vs 86 _._ 1). SPT Transformers surpass the performance of both trained from scratch and SPT versions of S4 on 3 out of 6 tasks. The results in Table 2 defy current understanding, with prior works citing the sub-par LRA performance of Transformers as a prime motivating factor for new methods. Yet we show that, while architectural developments indeed lead to remarkable performance gains, most of the priors essential to high performance can already be learned from data directly.

In case of S4, while SPT leads to modest gains on most tasks, a substantial gain of 20% is observed on the challenging PathX-256 task with input length of 65 _K_ , significantly improving over the best reported performance of 63 _._ 1% by (Dao et al., 2022) who, in addition, used extra data from the Pathfinder-64 task.

The additionally reported models, SPADE and MEGA, are Transformer variants that augment the model with a single or several state space layers. SPADE combines the outputs of a frozen S4 layer and local attention in the first block, while MEGA incorporates a learned exponential moving average, an instance of diagonal SSMs, into gated attention blocks. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to show that purely attention-based methods, without any architectural modifications, can achieve competitive results on LRA. While incorporating SSMs can be important in terms of scalability to longer sequences due to their log-linear complexity with respect to input length, we show that in terms of model performance, pretraining leads to biases that are as effective as manual designs.

An important aspect of SPT is the use of additional compute compared to the trained from scratch baseline and it is natural to investigate if similar gains can be obtained by training from scratch for longer. For all our trained from scratch baselines, we ensured that the validation performance had converged and did not improve for several consecutive epochs. We examine the aspect of the computational overhead of SPT in detail Appendix D, where we show that SPT leads to significant gains, even in the setting where the same amount of compute is used for SPT models and the ones that are trained from scratch.

The results are summarized in Figure 2 and per-task results are provided in Table 7. We find that, when trained from scratch, with both random and structured initializations, DLR lags behind S4 in terms of average performance (77 vs 83) demonstrating that biases incorporated through the specific initialization and parameterization used in S4 are indeed critical to performance. However, the picture radically changes under SPT – with SPT, DLR outperforms a trained from scratch S4 (83 _._ 4 vs 82 _._ 8) and is only slightly behind SPT S4 (83 _._ 4 vs 84 _._ 5). This suggests that the data-driven priors learned through pretraining are almost as effective as the manual biases incorporated in S4.

Results in this section have two additional implications. First, this is the first instance in which vanilla diagonal linear RNNs have been shown to achieve competitive performance on LRA. Prior work by Orvieto et al. (2023) suggested an additional normalization step in the kernel generation on top of a tailor-made initialization to achieve high performance on LRA. Second, while our discussion revolved around SSMs, many subsequent works on designing global convolutions followed similar principles. For example, Li et al. (2022) proposed to generate a decaying convolution kernel from shorter kernels via interpolation, which induces smoothness and can be viewed as a normalization step. Similarly, Fu et al. (2023) applied a global convolution layer that is transformed by a deterministic function to explicitly induce a smoother kernel. Yet our results suggest that these explicit steps are less significant when models are self pretrained.

## 3.4 SELF PRETRAINING IS EFFECTIVE ACROSS DATA SCALES

As the priors learned via SPT are data-driven, their efficacy is dependent on the training set itself, which leads us to examine the performance gains as a function of the dataset size. To this end, given a downstream task, we randomly sample a subset of the training set, and study the performance gains for S4 due to SPT under varying sizes of the subset. We restrict the pretraining phase of S4 to a fixed number of update steps across all experiments and finetune until convergence.

As summarized in Figure 3, we uncover an interesting phenomenon; while the relative gains from SPT over the trained from scratch baseline S4 are modest when the full task data is available, they become increasingly significant (and as large as 30%) on smaller data scales. This shows that priors from pretraining are especially effective when training data is scarce and, in the context of previous sections, implies that the incorporation of the pretraining stage is important for model evaluation regardless of dataset size. In Appendix E, we provide a complementary study on the effectiveness of SPT across model sizes, demonstrating that indeed SPT is effective across multiple model scales for both S4 and Transformers.

To answer this, we consider Pythia 70M (Biderman et al., 2023), an autoregressive Transformer pretrained on the Pile (Gao et al., 2020) as well as a randomly initialized version with the same architecture, denoted as “Pythia 70M (RandInit)” in Table 2. To be comparable to existing results and due to the formal requirements of the LRA benchmark, we use character/pixel-level tokenization instead of the original BPE tokenizer and the model is required to adapt to the new tokenization during finetuning.

As shown in Table 2, Pythia 70M generally lags behind our trained from scratch Transformer baseline due to the changed tokenization and difference between the pretraining distribution and the downstream tasks. This further highlights the importance of SPT as it allows the model to specifically learn and adapt to the structure and modality of the given task data. However, the performance of Pythia 70M is significantly better that its randomly initialized version Pythia 70M (Rand init) suggesting that pretraining on large text corpora can be beneficial across modalities.

## 3.6 THEORETICALLY-DERIVED VS DATA-DRIVEN KERNELS

Many high performing models such as SSMs incorporate manually-crafted priors to bias the model towards learning long range dependencies. For example, the initializations used in SSMs such as S4, DSS, S4D, S5 are based on HiPPO theory (Gu et al., 2020), which explicitly determines the decay rate of the convolution kernels over time and provides strong dependence between distant elements in the input sequence. In similar spirit, Li et al. (2022) generate convolution kernels modified with fixed weights aimed towards a slow decay. On the other hand, kernels learned via SPT have no guarantees of a slow decay and depend solely on the input distribution and the pretraining objective.

In this section, we analyze the structure of the convolutional kernels learned via SPT and compare them to the HiPPO-based kernels used to initialize existing SSMs such as S4. The convolution operation in S4 has the form

We generate kernels for the pretrained S4 models from Section 3.2 (before finetuning) and compare with the ones used in standard S4. Figure 4 plots _K_ max for the Image, Text, PathX and ListOps, all entailing better performance with the pretrained model (Table 2). We observe that the learned kernels exhibit variable decay rates across the tasks and model layers, in contrast to the fixed decay rate of the data-agnostic HiPPO kernels. In particular, on the Text task the learned kernels are more local compared to HiPPO. For PathX, the vertical grid lines are aligned with the image resolution (128 _×_ 128) showing high correlation between the underlying 2D structure of the data and the kernel peaks. Overall, Figure 4 further highlights the utility of SPT over data-agnostic initializations that cannot adapt to a local or global structure in a task distribution.

## 3.7 ADDITIONAL EXPERIMENTS

In addition to LRA, we also tested the utility of SPT on 3 additional datasets, encompassing 2 additional natural data modalities, described as follows:
- **Speech Commands (SC)** Raw speech waveforms of length 16 _K_ used in a 35-way classification task (Warden, 2018). We test both causal and bidirectional models following Gu et al. (2022b).
- **sCIFAR** Sequential CIFAR-10 dataset using RGB channels as features. This is similar to the Image task from LRA that uses grayscale images, except that here richer features are used.
- **BIDMC** A suite of 3 regression tasks, requiring to predict respiratory rate (RR), heart rate (HR) and blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) from EKG and PPG signals of length 4 _K_ each.

The results shown in Table 3, with additional details in Appendix C.3, further strengthen the claims made throughout this work. On both SC and sCIFAR tasks, SPT leads to large performance gains for Transformers and modest gains for S4. The gaps between trained from scratch Transformer and S4 are substantially narrowed with SPT. On the SC task, SPT leads to a large 5% improvement for Transformers and we observe the performance gap between causal and bidirectional variants of S4 to be mitigated with SPT. A similar, but not identical, observation is made in section 3.1 where masked and causal SPT lead to very similar results on all LRA tasks.

On the sCIFAR task, SPT leads to a dramatic 11% improvement for Transformers, nearly matching the performance of S4 (90 _._ 3 vs 91 _._ 7) and again pointing towards a sub-optimal evaluation when only training from scratch. On BIDMC, the performances of both Transformer and S4 baselines are already close to perfect and it is hard to observe any meaningful improvements due to SPT.

In general, our results suggest that similar under-estimation of model performances might also be prevalent in other scenarios where training from scratch is standard (Deletang´ et al., 2022; Velivckovi’c et al., 2022; Dwivedi et al., 2022).

## A RELATED WORK

**Modeling Long Range Dependencies** Evaluation of long-sequence models commonly includes the LRA benchmark (Tay et al., 2020a), a suite of tasks demonstrating the inefficacy of various efficient Transformers on long sequences,(Tay et al., 2020b). The first to obtain high performance on LRA was the S4 model (Gu et al., 2022a), an instance of linear RNNs augmented according to a complementary theory in continuous time (Gu et al., 2020; 2022b). Following S4, multiple works have proposed simplifications to S4 (Gupta et al., 2022a; Gu et al., 2022b; Smith et al., 2023; Orvieto et al., 2023) or augmentations to other common architectures (Li et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2022; Zuo et al., 2022), aimed at replicating biases observed in S4 and achieving similar performance on LRA. Common to all of the above is that evaluation on the tasks is done from a random initialization and thus does not encompass the biases from a various pretraining objectives, and specifically denoising. In Gupta et al. (2022b), the authors examine diagonal linear RNNs and Transformers on a set of long range tasks with dense (token-level) supervision but do not evaluate on LRA.

**Pretraining with Downstream Data** Pretraining prior to evaluation is already the best practice in many domains and is usually performed using upstream corpora that are much larger than the intended downstream task. While various objectives could be used for pretraining (Lewis et al., 2020; Baevski et al., 2020; Assran et al., 2023) we focused on the typical causal and masked language modeling objectives (Radford et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). El-Nouby et al. (2021) were the first to show that self-supervised pretraining on the downstream task data alone can often match performance of supervised ImageNet pretraining, for object detection and segmentation tasks. In Krishna et al. (2023), the authors provide a similar study on NLP tasks, showing multiple instances in which pretraining on the task data performs comparably to off-the-shelf models pretrained on large text corpora.

## B CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we argued against the common practice of training models from scratch to evaluate their performance on long range tasks and suggested an efficient and effective solution to mitigate this issue – self-supervised pretraining on the task data itself. Through a comprehensive array of experiments, we showed that our method consistently leads to dramatically improved performance for multiple architectures across data scales, and allows simpler models to match the performance of complex ones. Our work stresses the need to account for the pretraining stage while designing and evaluating novel architectures in the future.

## C APPENDIX

## C.1 ADDITIONAL EXPERIMENT DETAILS & HYPER-PARAMETERS

We provide additional implementation notes and hyper-parameters for reproduction purposes. For all experiments listed in the main text, pretraining is performed either with cross-entropy (CE) or L1 loss, and the normalization layer for Transformers always uses LayerNorm in the PreNorm setting. When finetuning a pretrained model we use the checkpoint matching highest validation accuracy / R2 score for CE / L1 loss respectively, and perform a search over a small grid of learning rates and batch sizes with log spacing, e.g. (1e-3 5e-4 1e-4) and (8 32 128), with values for best performance reported in Table C.1.

Our experiments were performed on NVIDIA 3090 and V100 GPUs. All models were trained for a maximum of either 200 epochs or 24h on a single GPU (whichever comes first) for pre-training and fine-tuning, with exceptions for Transformers on PathX & Speech Commands experiments that were trained for a maximum of 5 days on 4 GPUs, and Pythia experiments that were trained for a maximum of 2 days on 4 GPUs. When detailing learning rates for State Space models, learning rates for parameters of the State Space layer itself are set differently to predefined values, we always use values provided by respective authors (Gu et al., 2022a; Gupta et al., 2022b).
Additional specifications such as learning rate schedules and optimizers can be found in our repository.

## C.1.1 UNDERESTIMATION OF LONG-RANGE ABILITIES OF TRANSFORMERS

Model hyper-parameters (e.g. model size, num. layers, num. attention heads etc.) and model configuration (classification head, normalization layer type etc.) are similar to those listed in Appendix A in (Tay et al., 2020a) and provided here in Table 4, with the main exception being the pooling method across sequence dimension during finetuning. We use mean-pooling, max-pooling or the last element of the sequence, instead of using a [CLS] token, as done in Tay et al. (2020a). The choice of pooling method for SPT models had most impact on the PathX tasks, but varying across max/mean/last pooling with trained-from-scratch Transformers did not exceed random performance, same as Tay et al. (2020b).

## C.1.2 COMPARING S4 AND TRANSFORMERS

As listed in the main text, in this section there are discrepancies between model sizes of Transformers and S4. For Text & ListOps, the models in official repository of Gu et al. (2022a) are smaller then the Transformers used in LRA, we tried larger S4 models (from scratch and SPT) matching the Transformer in parameter count which did not result in better performance. For hyper-parameters of S4 models we refer to Gu et al. (2022a) - Appendix D.2 Table 11, and Table 5 for results of our grid searches. Hyper-parameters of Transformers can be found in Table 4. For the PathX-256 task we use the same configuration as PathX, changing the pooling method again from Mean to Max. For pretraining we use learning rate 1e-3, batch size 8 and batch size 64 for fine tuning with the same learning rate, the trained from scratch model’s learning rate is 1e-4 with batch size 16.

## C.1.3 THE ROLE OF EXPLICIT PRIORS

S4 models follow the setup described in C.1.2 for structured and random initialization, with results for grid searches in Table 5. For DLR (Gupta et al., 2022b), we use the official implementation[4] , and set the State Space layer parameter as the default, with state size 1024. For the remaining hyper-parameters (model size, num. layers, weight decay) we use the same values as S4, with the exception of the normalization layer that is fixed to LayerNorm. For the random initialization, we reset all model parameter value to random normal values and standard deviation 0.1. For all experiments on PathX we use Max-pooling on the final sequence representation, which we found helpful for optimization.

## C.1.4 SELF PRETRAINING IS EFFECTIVE ACROSS DATA SCALES

Experiments in this section use similar hyper-parameters to Table 5. For data restriction, we use fractions of the entire training set: (Num. Train Samples) _× {_ 0 _._ 005 _,_ 0 _._ 01 _,_ 0 _._ 05 _,_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 1 _._ 0 _}_ . The subsets are inclusive, meaning smaller fractions are contained in bigger ones, to avoid biases from difficult sampled subsets. For experiments on Text we pretrain all models for 40 _K_ pretraining steps and finetune the checkpoint with best performance on the validation set until convergence. For Image we pretrain all models for 10 _K_ step and finetune similarly. The learning rate and batch sizes are fixed across all subsample experiments, on Text we use batch size 8 and learning rate 5e-4 for pretraining and 16, 5e-4 for finetuning, for the trained from scratch model we use batch size 16 and learning rates 1e-3, 5e-4 and use the model with best performance on the validation set. For Image we use batch size 50 for all experiments, learning rate 1e-2 for pretraining, and learning rate to 2e-3 for finetuning and training from scratch.

## C.3 ADDITIONAL EXPERIMENTS - DETAILS

For experiments in this section we follow the scheme described in Section 2 for the experimental setup. Namely, for masked pretraining we use a fixed masking ratio for each task, for SC and BIDMC we use 25% masking and for sCIFAR we use 50%. In the SC task, when a causal model is also trained, we pretrain with a causal denoising objective. Due to the significant length of SC sequences, 16 _K_ , we split the input to the attention layer into non-overlapping blocks of size 4000 and allow each block to attend to itself and its neighbour(s), in similar fashion to experiments on PathX.

In BIDMC, we observed that naive finetuning of pretrained models results in very bad performance, which is an artifact of the label and data scales being very different, e.g. common label values for the HR task are _∼_ 80 while input is normalized to [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1]. The reported performance is after normalizing the labels to lie in [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1], which does not entail any additional information on the label itself.

## D COMPUTE REQUIREMENTS OF SELF PRETRAINING

In sections 3.1, 3.2 and 3.7 we observed large empirical gains using SPT compared to simply training models from scratch. However, as SPT requires additional compute, it is important to ensure that the reported gains are not an artifact of additional resources and that training longer from scratch does not result in similar gains.
In each of our trained from scratch (TFS) baseline experiments, we ensured that either (1) the training accuracy is almost perfect and validation performance stopped improving for multiple epochs, or

(2) the training loss stopped reducing. E.g. TFS Transformer on PathX fail to exceed 52% training accuracy after 8 epochs (equivalent to 2 days on 4 V100 GPUs), while SPT for 1 day and finetuning for 1 day achieved training accuracy _≥_ 78%. Across our experiments, we observed case (2) to occur on a small number of runs and case (1) to be the dominant paradigm.

This implies that the gains due to SPT cannot be simply explained by the use of additional compute and possible underfitting of TFS models, but rather by improved generalization as a consequence of the pretraining denoising objective.

To validate this further, we conducted a compute-tied study for the Image and Text tasks from LRA, where we fixed the total number of training epochs across SPT and fineutning (FT) and varied the number of epochs allocated for SPT[5] . progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a provocative and methodologically sound challenge to the current paradigm of benchmarking long-sequence models. The authors argue convincingly that the perceived superiority of specialized architectures like State Space Models (SSMs) over Transformers on benchmarks such as Long Range Arena (LRA) is largely an artifact of suboptimal "from-scratch" initialization. By introducing "self-pretraining" (SPT)—training on downstream data with a denoising objective—the paper demonstrates that vanilla Transformers can match SSM performance, and SSMs themselves can reach new state-of-the-art levels (e.g., +20 points on PathX-256). The quality of the work is high, particularly in its potential to simplify the architectural landscape by showing that data-driven priors can replace complex, hand-crafted parameterizations. However, the most significant issue for the authors to address in the full manuscript is the risk of "pretraining on the test signal." While SPT uses only downstream data, if the denoising objective implicitly solves the core difficulty of the task (e.g., spatial connectivity in PathX), the authors must clarify whether they are measuring architectural capacity or simply the efficacy of the denoising objective itself. Additionally, the computational cost of the SPT phase should be explicitly compared to the "from-scratch" training time to ensure the "efficient" claim holds up under practical constraints. Overall, this is a high-impact paper that demands a re-evaluation of how the community measures inductive bias.

comments.explanation: The text defines the sequence length of PathX-256 as '256[2] = 65536'. In the context of 2D images flattened into 1D sequences (as described for Image and Pathfinder tasks), this value is clearly intended to be $256^2$. While the OCR has rendered the exponent as '[2]', a reader can infer the squaring operation from the result 65,536. However, later in the same section, the paper refers to PathX (the $128 \times 128$ version) as having a length of 16,384 ($128^2$). The notation $256[2]$ is non-standard and likely an OCR artifact, but more importantly, the '256' in the name PathX-256 refers to the side dimension, whereas in 'PathX' it does not. This is a minor point of potential confusion regarding how tasks are named versus how their lengths are calculated.

In the original LRA paper, 'Transformer' usually refers to a specific base architecture (often 2 layers, 64-128 hidden dim for the 'Vanilla' baseline). Because SPT involves a pretraining phase (denoising) and a finetuning phase, it isn't explicitly stated if 'comparable size' accounts for the potential overhead of a reconstruction head used during the Masked SPT phase, or if the internal hyperparameters (heads, d_model, layers) are identical. While likely identical, the term 'comparable' instead of 'identical' leaves room for slight parameter count deviations that could influence the 30% gain.

comments.title: Unclear Kernel Normalization in K_max Plot Description
comments.quote: Figure 4 plots $K$ max for the Image, Text, PathX and ListOps... We observe that the learned kernels exhibit variable decay rates across the tasks and model layers, in contrast to the fixed decay rate of the data-agnostic HiPPO kernels.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.6, the authors state they plot $K_{max}$ to compare learned kernels with HiPPO kernels. The summary defines $K_{max,l} = \max_c |K_{c,l}|$. However, in SSMs, the scale of the kernel $K$ is tied to the variance of the initialization of $\mathbf{C}$ and $\mathbf{B}$. When comparing a 'rapidly decaying' learned kernel to a 'slowly decaying' HiPPO kernel based on $K_{max}$ magnitude over time $l$, the relative scale (normalization) is crucial to determine if the 'decay' is a functional property of the state transition $\mathbf{A}$ or merely a result of smaller weight magnitudes in the output projection. The text mentions 'vertical grid lines are aligned with image resolution,' which is a strong qualitative observation, but doesn't specify if kernels were normalized by their initial value $K_0$ to make the 'decay rate' comparison fair across different training stages.

comments.quote: and to 10% for ListOps. ... (Table 1) Transformers + Masked SPT [Listops] 59.75
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors state the masking ratio for ListOps is set to 10%. However, in the 'Notation & Definitions' summary provided in the context, it mentions a 10% ratio for ListOps. When looking at the results in Table 1 for 'Transformers + Masked SPT', the model achieves 59.75% accuracy. In Section 3.1, the authors note that 'in the original data, the arguments to the list operations are sampled randomly, meaning that inferring missing tokens from the context is rarely possible.' This creates a logical tension: if the tokens are random and the masking ratio is only 10% (leaving 90% of the structure visible), the 'denoising' task might be too trivial to learn the deep hierarchical priors the authors claim are being learned (demonstrated by the jump from 36% to 59%). While not a mathematical error, the choice of a lower masking ratio for a task where they acknowledge 'inferring missing tokens is rarely possible' seems counter-intuitive compared to the 50% used for images, and the text does not explain why a 10% ratio was sufficient to trigger such a large performance jump on a 'random' dataset.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Block' Attention for Long Sequences
comments.quote: Due to computational constraints, for tasks with sequence length at least 16 K we split the input to the attention layer into non-overlapping blocks of size 4096 and allow each block to attend to itself and its neighbour(s).
comments.explanation: For PathX and PathX-256 (lengths 16k and 64k), the authors deviate from 'vanilla' full attention to a block-local version. While necessary for memory, this introduces an architectural inductive bias (local/sliding window attention) which conflicts with the claim in Section 3.1 and 3.2 that they achieve these results with 'no architectural changes' and 'purely attention-based methods'. More importantly, for the PathX-256 task where they report a massive 20-point gain (87.73), it is unclear if the 'neighbors' include both preceding and succeeding blocks (bidirectional) or just preceding (causal), and whether this block structure is maintained during finetuning. If the 'biases essential to high performance' are learned via SPT, but the attention mechanism itself is restricted to a local 4k-8k window on a 64k sequence, the paper should clarify how the model captures the 'extremely long-range spatial dependencies' mentioned in the summary, given that a block-based Transformer is no longer a 'vanilla' global Transformer.

comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, equation (3) defines the DLR by setting $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{1}$ (the all-ones vector). While this is a common simplification in some SSM papers to reduce parameter count, the original S4 and DLR papers usually keep $B$ as a learnable parameter ($\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times 1}$) even if the transition $A$ is diagonal. The authors claim in Section 3.3 that DLR is 'significantly simpler to compute compared to S4'. If $\mathbf{B}$ is fixed to $\mathbf{1}$, the model loses a degree of freedom in how the input $u_n$ enters the state space. I considered if this was an OCR misread of a bold $\mathbf{B}$, but the text explicitly says 'where $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones vector'. If the authors fixed $\mathbf{B}$ to a constant, it is a much stricter simplification than the standard DLR, which might explain why it performs poorly from scratch (Figure 2) compared to S4. This should be clarified as it affects the reproducibility of the 'simpler model' claims.

|Transformer + Masked SPT_†_|128|4|8|128|Mean|1e-3,1e-3|256,128|0|CE|
comments.explanation: In Table 4, under the 'Pathfnder' [sic] and 'PathX' categories, there are duplicate rows for 'Transformer + Masked SPT_†_' with significantly different learning rates and batch sizes (e.g., batch size 16 vs 256). While I consider whether these represent different experimental runs or sub-tasks, the text in C.1.1 and C.1.2 does not explicitly clarify why two sets of configurations are listed for the same model in the same task. This creates ambiguity for reproduction: it is unclear which configuration produced the results in Table 2 or if the results are an average of both. Given the '†' symbol denotes exceptions/specific results, the lack of distinction between these two rows is problematic.

comments.title: Conflicting Batch Size Descriptions for PathX-256
comments.quote: For pretraining we use learning rate 1e-3, batch size 8 and batch size 64 for fine tuning with the same learning rate, the trained from scratch model’s learning rate is 1e-4 with batch size 16.
comments.explanation: Section C.1.2 states that for PathX-256, the finetuning batch size is 64. However, Table 4 (which lists hyper-parameters for Transformers in all sections) shows the batch size for PathX as (32, 8) or (256, 8) for (FT, PT). If PathX-256 uses the 'same configuration as PathX' as claimed in the text, the jump to batch size 64 in the prose contradicts the values 32 or 256 in the table. This makes it difficult to determine the exact optimization setup used for the most challenging task in the paper.

comments.title: Potential Loss Function Mismatch for Image and SC Tasks
comments.quote: pretraining is performed either with cross-entropy (CE) or L1 loss... [Table 4] Image ... PT Loss L1 ... SC ... PT Loss L1
comments.explanation: The paper defines Masked SPT for visual/image tasks as a masked modeling objective. Typically, for discrete tokens (like pixels quantized or character-based LRA Image), Cross Entropy is used. Table 4 indicates L1 loss was used for Image (LRA), SC (Speech Commands), and sCIFAR (via Table 3 context). While L1 is standard for continuous regression (like BIDMC), its use for the LRA Image task (which uses pixel values 0-255 often treated as classification or normalized intensity) is unusual if the goal is 'denoising' as described in the summary. If the pixels are treated as continuous values, L1 is plausible, but the text fluctuates between calling it 'Masked SPT' (suggesting BERT-like token masking) and using regression losses without explaining the output head adaptation for these specific modalities.

comments.title: Hyper-parameter formatting inconsistency in Table 5
comments.explanation: The legend for Table 5 states that values are reported in the format (FT-LR, FT-BSZ, PT-LR, PT-BSZ). For 'S4 + Rand Init + SPT' under the 'Text' column, only three values are provided ('1e-3, 16, 32'). Based on the four-parameter template, it is unclear if the missing value is the PT-LR or PT-BSZ, or if one of the values (like 16 or 32) is intended to apply to both phases. This occurs again for 'DLR + Rand Init + SPT' in the 'Image' column ('1e-3, 64, 32'). While a reader might guess that the learning rate is shared, the lack of a fourth digit in a strictly defined 4-tuple format creates ambiguity for exact replication of the pretraining vs. finetuning setup.

comments.title: Conflicting Hyper-parameters for S4+SPT across Tables 5 and 6
comments.explanation: Table 6 lists hyper-parameters for SSMs in Section 3.7 (Speech Commands, sCIFAR, BIDMC). However, the row labeled 'S4' in Table 6 contains values for 'SC-Causal' and 'SC-Bi.' that appear to be exact duplicates of the 'Listops' and 'Text' values for 'S4+SPT' from Table 5. For example, SC-Causal lists (1e-3, 16, 1e-3, 128), which is identical to the Listops entry in Table 5. Given that SC (16k length) and Listops (2k length) are vastly different tasks, and the text in C.1.6 suggests these should be grid-searched or taken from Gu et al. (2022a), it's highly probable that some values in Table 6 were accidentally copy-pasted from the rows of Table 5 during formatting, rather than representing the actual speech task parameters.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Compute-Tied' definition for S4
comments.quote: We fix the total number of training epochs across SPT and fineutning (FT) and varied the number of epochs allocated for SPT. ... 0% (TFS) 75.04 87.83 79.08 87.51 (Table 8)
comments.explanation: The authors aim to prove SPT's efficiency by showing that even when restricted to the same total epoch budget as a 'TFS' (Train From Scratch) model, SPT wins. However, for the S4 model in Table 2 (the main results), the Paper Summary and footnotes indicate that S4 TFS results are often 'cited from the original work'. In Table 8, the S4 TFS (0% SPT) performance on Image is 87.83. If the total budget for Image is 150 epochs, and S4 usually requires specific schedules to converge, it's unclear if the 'TFS' baseline in this compute-tied comparison is the same optimized S4 baseline from the original paper or a version the authors retrained with a potentially sub-optimal restricted budget. Footnote 5 acknowledges 'different from Table 2', but does not clarify if the TFS baseline was also subject to the same 150/30 epoch constraint, which is critical to the 'compute-tied' claim.

comments.title: Model Scale Magnitude Inconsistency
comments.quote: evaluate both S4 and a Transformer with rotary positional embeddings with model sizes spanning four orders of
comments.explanation: The text states that the model sizes span 'four orders of magnitude'. However, looking at the header of Table 9, the model sizes listed are 100K, 300K, 1M, 3M, and 10M. These values span from $10^5$ to $10^7$, which is only two orders of magnitude (a factor of 100). Even if the authors are counting the number of data points (5), the range itself does not cover four orders ($10^4$). This is likely a hyperbolic or oversight error in the prose description of the experimental setup compared to the actual parameters reported in the table.

comments.title: Unexplained Performance Plateau in Transformer Baseline
comments.explanation: In Table 9, the performance for the 'Transformer+Rotary' (trained from scratch) is identical for both the 100K and 300K model sizes (68.51). While it is possible for two different model scales to yield the same result by chance, a 'thoughtful reviewer' might suspect a copy-paste error in the table generation, especially given that the 'Transformer+Rotary + Masked SPT' row shows a clear progression (74.43 to 76.36) over those same scales. If 68.51 is the 'chance' accuracy or a local optimum for the Image task at low parameter counts, it should be clarified; otherwise, it appears to be a numerical inconsistency.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and timely contribution to the ongoing discourse on architectural evaluation in long-sequence modeling, making a strong case against the standard practice of training and comparing models from random initialization. The central claim—that data-driven priors via self-pretraining (SPT) on downstream task data alone drastically reduce performance gaps between architectures, particularly between Transformers and State Space Models (SSMs) like S4—is supported by rigorous experimentation and a well-motivated design. The clarity of the argument is a notable strength: the authors effectively frame the discrepancy between the poor performance of Transformers on synthetic long-range benchmarks (e.g., LRA) and their strong real-world performance on long-sequence tasks as a methodological artifact of inadequate training protocols rather than an architectural limitation. The empirical results are striking, especially the 20-point improvement on PathX-256 and the demonstration that vanilla Transformers can match S4 when pretrained, which challenges widely accepted conclusions in the literature. However, the paper would benefit from a more complete presentation of the ablation studies, particularly regarding the specific components of SSMs (e.g., structured state matrices, initialization schemes) that become redundant post-SPT, and how these findings generalize across different denoising objectives or sequence lengths. Additionally, while the methodological message is powerful, the analysis of learned convolution kernels—promised as a mechanism for understanding *how* SPT induces beneficial priors—is cut off mid-sentence in the submitted text, raising concerns about the completeness of one of the paper’s key analytical contributions. Assuming the full analysis is included in the final version, this work has the potential to significantly shift evaluation norms in the field, advocating for more realistic and fair architectural comparisons through simple, data-efficient pretraining.

comments.title: Inconsistent representation of average performance in Figure 1 and Table 2
comments.explanation: Figure 1 juxtaposes average LRA performance (e.g., 86.21 for Transformers + SPT) with a specific task result (87.11 for PathX-256), without clear dual-axis labeling. While the caption refers to 'Mean performance - LRA PathX-256', this is ambiguous. The values are accurate and match Table 2 (Transformers + SPT avg: 86.21; S4 + SPT on PathX-256: 87.11), but the visual implies these are comparable metrics on the same scale, potentially misleading readers about the magnitude of improvement. The figure should distinguish between aggregated averages and individual task results using separate axes or explicit annotations to prevent misinterpretation.

comments.title: Overstated claim of 20 absolute point improvement on PathX-256
comments.quote: we improve the best reported results of SSMs on the PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points
comments.explanation: The paper claims a 20-point improvement over the 'best reported results of SSMs' on PathX-256. However, the baseline cited in Table 2 is S4 (Gu et al., 2022a) at 67.82%, and S4 + SPT achieves 87.11%, a difference of 19.29 points. While this may be rounded to 20, the phrase '20 absolute points' is slightly exaggerated. More critically, the text later references Dao et al. (2022)’s prior result of 63.1%, suggesting a 24-point gain, which conflates two different baselines: (1) the improvement due to SPT (from 67.82 to 87.11), and (2) prior work. This risks misleading readers into attributing the full 24-point gain to SPT, when part stems from architectural advances. The claim is factually inaccurate in asserting a 20-point gain over the best SSM result (67.82), and the conflation with weaker baselines exaggerates the novelty.

comments.title: Ambiguous and underspecified definition of 'structured initialization' for DLR
comments.quote: (2) “structured” initialization recommended by the respective authors aimed at capturing long-range dependencies.
comments.explanation: The paper contrasts random and structured initialization for both S4 and DLR in Figure 2 and Section 3.3 but fails to define what 'structured initialization' means for DLR. For S4, it references the principled HiPPO-based initialization for slow-decaying kernels, which is standard. However, DLR lacks the Cauchy parameterization and structured state space of S4, so the transferability and implementation of 'structured' initialization are unclear. The text cites no source or equation for DLR’s structured init, and while Gupta et al. (2022b) may propose one, this is not referenced. This omission undermines reproducibility and weakens the experimental claim that SPT makes manual priors redundant, as the baseline (structured init) is not clearly defined. The term 'structured' is used vaguely without methodological transparency.

comments.title: Faulty claim that SPT Transformers surpass SPT versions of S4 on 3 out of 6 tasks
comments.quote: SPT Transformers surpass the performance of both trained from scratch and SPT versions of S4 on 3 out of 6 tasks.
comments.explanation: This statement is factually incorrect. According to Table 2, SPT Transformers (Transformers + Rotary + Masked SPT) outperform non-SPT S4 (Gu et al., 2022a) on 3 tasks: ListOps, Text, and Retrieval. However, they do **not** surpass S4 + SPT on any task: Image (86.04 vs 88.65), Pathfinder (94.16 vs 94.92), PathX (92.98 vs 96.94), and PathX-256 (87.11 vs 67.82, but same model). The average also shows S4 + SPT (86.75) > SPT Transformers (86.21). The claim falsely states that SPT versions of S4 are surpassed, which they are not. The authors likely intended to say 'surpass non-SPT S4 and approach SPT-S4 performance', but the current phrasing is a significant overstatement that misrepresents the comparative results.

comments.title: Ambiguous and misleading phrasing of PathX-256 sequence length notation
comments.quote: we examine an additional variant of PathX called PathX-256 with sequence length 256[2] = 65536
comments.explanation: The expression '256[2]' is nonstandard and confusing—likely an OCR artifact or notation error. It is intended to convey that PathX-256 corresponds to a 256×256 image flattened into a sequence, hence 65,536 elements. However, '256[2]' is not a conventional mathematical or programming notation. It could be misinterpreted as an array index or typo. Given that PathX is 16,384 (128×128), context supports the 256×256 interpretation. Still, this ambiguity risks confusion, especially for readers unfamiliar with image serialization in sequence models. The notation should be revised to '256×256' or '256²' for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent or underspecified hyperparameter reporting in appendix tables
comments.explanation: Multiple issues affect hyperparameter reporting in Table 5: (1) Some entries (e.g., '1e-3,16,32') contain only three values despite expecting four (FT-LR, FT-BSZ, PT-LR, PT-BSZ), suggesting a formatting or input error; (2) In Table 4, learning rates like '1e-3,1e03' for Image (where '1e03' = 1000) are implausibly high and likely OCR errors; (3) Section C.1.4 states pretraining LR for Image is 1e-2, conflicting with Table 4 values; (4) The order of LR pairs (FT vs PT) is ambiguous without clear column headers. These inconsistencies compromise reproducibility. While some may stem from OCR, the pattern suggests broader methodological underspecification. The authors should clarify hyperparameter conventions, define ordering, verify table values, and align prose with table data.

comments.title: Underspecified pretraining setup for sCIFAR and ListOps
comments.quote: for masked pretraining we use a fixed masking ratio for each task, for SC and BIDMC we use 25% masking and for sCIFAR we use 50%.
comments.explanation: While masking ratios are stated, the pretraining objective for sCIFAR is underspecified: are pixels masked individually, in blocks, or as patches? Is the model causal or bidirectional? For ListOps, the masking ratio is set to 10% without citation or justification, unlike other tasks. Given that ListOps is structurally sensitive and symbolic, the lower ratio may be reasonable, but no rationale is offered. Similarly, for sCIFAR (based on CIFAR-10), the method does not specify how images are tokenized or whether masking follows vision transformer conventions. These omissions hinder reproduction, particularly since SPT yields large gains on these tasks. The paper references Section 2, but the provided context lacks these details.

comments.title: Missing or unjustified experimental details affecting interpretation
comments.quote: Despite the efficiency of SPT presented in Table 8, the performance still lags behind the unrestricted setting suggesting.
comments.explanation: This issue encompasses several unresolved methodological gaps: (1) 'Unrestricted setting' is undefined—does it mean more compute, longer training, or different hyperparameters? Without clarification, the claim that SPT 'lags' may mislead readers into thinking it is less effective or efficient, contradicting the paper’s thesis; (2) The pooling method for PathX is listed as 'Max' in Table 4, yet the text claims it was changed from 'Mean to Max' only for PathX-256, implying inconsistency; (3) Loss functions for S4/DLR pretraining are unspecified, though critical for self-supervised learning. These omissions reduce experimental transparency and hinder fair comparison and replication.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)

Assuming the full analysis is included in the final version, this work has the potential to significantly shift evaluation norms in the field, advocating for more realistic and fair architectural comparisons through simple, data-efficient pretraining.

comments.title: Inconsistent average in Figure 1 labels
comments.explanation: At first glance, the numbers in Figure 1 (86.21, 86.09, [86.75], 87.11) appear to label performance values, likely corresponding to Transformer SPT, S4 from scratch, S4 SPT, and PathX-256 S4 SPT. However, the placement and labeling are ambiguous. The caption says 'Mean performance - LRA PathX-256', but 'mean performance' and 'PathX-256' are mutually exclusive unless it’s a dual-axis plot. Looking at Table 2, Transformers + Masked SPT have an average of 86.21 (excluding PathX-256), S4 (scratch) avg is 86.09, S4 + SPT avg is 86.75, and S4 + SPT on PathX-256 is 87.11. So the numbers align with those results. However, plotting both average LRA performance and a single-task result (PathX-256) on the same axis without clear dual labeling is misleading. While the values themselves are accurate, the juxtaposition suggests that 86.21 and 87.11 are directly comparable scores on the same task, when one is an average and the other is a specific task result. This could mislead a reader into misinterpreting the scale of improvement. The figure could be clarified with separate axes or labels to distinguish aggregated vs. individual task metrics.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed 20-point improvement and referenced value in PathX-256
comments.quote: we improve the best reported results of SSMs on the PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points
comments.explanation: The abstract and main text claim a '20 absolute point' improvement on PathX-256. Table 2 shows S4 + SPT achieves 87.11, while S4 (scratch) is at 67.82, a difference of 19.29, which rounds to ~19.3. Later, the text says 'a substantial gain of 20% is observed', which is ambiguous: '20%' could mean relative or absolute. But in the abstract, '20 absolute points' is clear. The math (87.11 - 67.82 = 19.29) contradicts this. However, in Section 3.2, the authors clarify that their S4 scratch baseline (67.82) improves over a prior best of 63.1% (Dao et al., 2022) — so perhaps they mean 20 points over prior published results (67.82 → 87.11 is ~19.3, but 63.1 → 87.11 is 24.01). That interpretation would make the claim accurate. But the sentence says 'improve the best reported results of SSMs', and 67.82 is already an SSM result (from Gu et al., 2022a) — so the best previously reported SSM result is 67.82, not 63.1. Hence, the 20-point claim overstates the improvement by ~0.7 points. Even if rounded, '20 absolute points' is slightly exaggerated. A careful reader might accept 'nearly 20 points', but as written, it’s a minor overstatement.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of DLR initialization in text vs. figure
comments.quote: For each model, we experiment with two sets of initializations: (1) random initialization where the state space parameters are initialized from a normal distribution with a small standard deviation, and
comments.explanation: The passage cuts off, but in context, it introduces two initialization schemes: random and structured. Figure 2 shows four bars: 'DLR Random Init', 'S4 Random Init', 'DLR Structured Init', 'S4 Structured Init', with average accuracy labels (77.33, 80.47, 82.75, 84.47). The text says that S4 uses a principled initialization for slow-decaying kernels, but it does not define what 'structured initialization' means for DLR. Given that DLR is a simpler model without the Cauchy parameterization of S4, it's unclear whether the 'structured' init for DLR is mimicking S4’s init or using some other scheme. The lack of specification makes reproduction difficult. The caption refers to Table 7 (not provided), so this may be detailed there. But within the provided text, the method is underspecified: what exactly is the 'structured initialization' for DLR? Is it the same as S4's? If so, is that even valid, given DLR's diagonal form? This could mislead a reader into assuming symmetric treatment of initialization that may not be justified. While the figure suggests structured init helps both, the implementation details are missing in the excerpt.

comments.title: Inconsistency in LRA task count and averaging convention
comments.quote: Average performance (“Avg.”) is reported without PathX-256 to align with prior work
comments.explanation: This statement in Table 2 is clear and justified: the standard LRA average excludes PathX-256 because it wasn’t part of the original benchmark. However, in Table 1, the average for 'Transformers + Masked SPT' is 81.34, and the per-task values sum to (59.75 + 89.27 + 88.64 + 74.22 + 88.45 + 87.73) / 6 ≈ 81.34, which includes PathX (not PathX-256). The confusion arises because 'PathX' in LRA is the 16K-length task, while PathX-256 is the 64K-length variant the authors introduce. But in Table 1, PathX is included in the average, contradicting the claim that averages exclude PathX-256. However, this is not an error: PathX (16K) is part of the original LRA; PathX-256 (64K) is the new one. So including PathX in the average is correct. The note applies only to PathX-256. The potential for confusion is high due to similar names. A reader might misread 'PathX' and 'PathX-256' as the same task. While technically consistent, the naming is ambiguous and risks misinterpretation. However, since the paper clearly defines PathX-256 as a new variant, and Table 1 includes only standard tasks, the inconsistency is only apparent, not actual.

comments.title: Missing definition of SSM recurrence equations
comments.quote: Given an input scalar sequence[2] _u_ , SSMs follow a linear recurrence generating a hidden state vector _⃗_ _**x** n_ at timestep _n_ , and produce a scalar output sequence _y_ as
comments.explanation: The text introduces the SSM recurrence but cuts off before presenting the actual equations (likely eq. 1 and 2 referenced later). This is a forward reference issue — the equations exist in the full paper but are not in the provided context. As per instructions, we do not flag missing equations if they are referenced and likely appear later. The derivation of the kernel _K_k = C A^k B_ is standard in SSM literature (e.g., in Gu et al., 2022a), so a reader familiar with the field would not be confused. The use of _K_k = C A^k B_ is correctly described as the impulse response of a linear dynamical system. No technical error is present in what is shown; the description is appropriate for an introductory overview. Therefore, no issue remains once contextual continuity is assumed.

comments.title: Potential confusion between causal and masked objectives in visual tasks
comments.quote: Both causal and masked pretraining yield similar results even in cases where there are no clear benefits to using a causal model, such as on the visual tasks
comments.explanation: The claim that causal (autoregressive) modeling works as well as masked (denoising) on visual tasks like Image and Pathfinder is surprising because images lack a natural temporal order, and autoregressive models typically rely on sequential generation. However, in the context of 1D flattened sequences (as stated in Section 2), positional information is preserved, and causal modeling can still exploit spatial dependencies. He et al. (2022) showed that self-pretraining with masked modeling works well for images; here, the authors say even causal modeling performs comparably. This is a notable empirical finding, not a logical error. The result is plausible because the autoregressive objective still forces the model to learn local and long-range structures. The paper does not claim superiority, just similarity. Given that this is an experimental result reported in Table 1 (where both objectives perform similarly), the statement is justified by data. No contradiction or overstatement is present.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of PathX-256 sequence length
comments.quote: we examine an additional variant of PathX called PathX-256 with sequence length 256[2] = 65536
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expression '256[2]' is confusing and appears to be a notation error or OCR artifact. Given that the final value is correctly stated as 65,536, which is 2^16, and since PathX is described as having sequence length 16,384 (2^14), it is plausible that the authors mean a variant where the sequence length is scaled by a factor related to 256. However, '256[2]' is nonstandard and ambiguous — it does not clearly convey exponentiation or dimensionality. A reasonable interpretation is that PathX-256 refers to a 256x256 image treated as a 1D sequence, hence 256^2 = 65,536. This aligns with the image flattening convention described for the Image task. The context supports this interpretation (visual tasks are flattened), and Figure 2 shows PathX-256 with 87.11 performance, confirming it as a meaningful long-sequence task. Still, the notation '256[2]' is misleading and not properly defined. It should be clarified as '256 × 256' or '256²' to avoid confusion. Since this could mislead a careful reader about the nature of the input scaling, it qualifies as an ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'PathX' and 'Pathfnder' in tables vs. text
comments.quote: |**Pathfnder**|**PathX**|
comments.explanation: The table uses 'Pathfnder' while the main text consistently writes 'Pathfinder'. Similarly, 'PathX' is used both in text and tables, but the OCR has likely dropped the 'i' in 'Finder'. This appears clearly to be an OCR artifact — the context confirms that 'Pathfnder' refers to the Pathfinder task, as defined in Section 2 with sequence length 1024. The spelling 'Pathfnder' occurs multiple times in tables (Table 1 and Table 2), but never in the prose, which uses 'Pathfinder'. Given that 'PathX' is also a variant of the same task family, the distinction is maintained. Since the meaning is recoverable from context and standard use in the field (LRA benchmark tasks), and the numbers align with known baselines, this is not a substantive error. However, for consistency and clarity in an academic paper, especially one comparing fine-grained performance, such discrepancies should be cleaned. But per instructions, we do not flag formatting or OCR-induced capitalization/spelling if intent is clear. Hence, this issue is noted but not flagged as problematic beyond OCR noise.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed 20-point gain and table values in PathX-256
comments.quote: improve upon the best reported accuracy on the challenging PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points (67 → 87)
comments.explanation: The paper claims a 20-point absolute improvement on PathX-256 (67 → 87), and Table 2 reports S4 (trained from scratch) at 67.82 and S4 + SPT at 87.11, which supports the claim. The use of '67 → 87' is a reasonable rounding for emphasis. However, the phrase 'best reported accuracy' is slightly ambiguous: earlier in Section 2, the authors state 'we are the first to report strong results on this task', suggesting no prior reports. Yet they cite 67.82 as the baseline, which they attribute to Gu et al. (2022a) with a † mark. This implies they are comparing against their own S4 baseline rather than an external prior result. The clarification in Table 2 resolves the concern — the gain is internal to their study, but the phrasing 'best reported accuracy' could overstate novelty if taken to mean prior art. However, since they explicitly reference S4 (Gu et al., 2022a) and their own result is in Table 2, the claim is justified in context. The '→' notation is informal but clear. No actual inconsistency remains, but the wording could tempt a reader to believe an external SOTA was surpassed by 20 points when it's an ablation. Still, the claim is technically accurate, so no flag is raised.

comments.title: Unclear justification for masking ratios across modalities
comments.quote: For the visual tasks (Image, Pathfinder, Path-X) the masking ratio for masked sequence modeling is set to 50% following He et al. (2022), to 15% for language tasks (Text, Retrieval) following Liu et al. (2019), and to 10% for ListOps.
comments.explanation: The authors state they follow prior work for masking ratios, which is reasonable. However, the choice for ListOps (10%) lacks a citation, unlike the others. ListOps is a syntactic task with structured inputs, so a lower masking ratio may be intended to preserve structure, but no justification is given. The context doesn't clarify why 10% is chosen — perhaps due to the low sequence length or semantic sensitivity. This is a minor underspecification: while reproduction is possible (the values are given), the rationale for differing ratios across tasks is not explained. In a methods section, especially when contrasting modalities, a brief justification would strengthen reproducibility and intent. However, since the core argument of SPT doesn't hinge on the exact masking ratio, and values are set based on established work for most tasks, this does not rise to a major issue. Still, for a full reproduction, one might question whether ablation over masking ratios was performed. The omission is minor but qualifies as underspecification.

comments.title: Potential Mismatch Between Claimed and Actual SPT Gain on S4 for PathX-256
comments.quote: a substantial gain of 20% is observed on the challenging PathX-256 task with input length of 65 _K_ , significantly improving over the best reported performance of 63 _._ 1% by (Dao et al., 2022)
comments.explanation: At first glance, the passage claims a '20% accuracy improvement' for S4 with SPT on PathX-256 compared to training from scratch. From Table 2, we see that S4 (trained from scratch) achieves 67.82% and S4 + Masked SPT achieves 87.11%, implying an 87.11 - 67.82 = 19.29 absolute percentage point gain, which the authors correctly round to ~20 absolute points (as confirmed in the PAPER SUMMARY). However, the phrasing 'a substantial gain of 20%' is ambiguous: it could be misread as a relative improvement (e.g. 20% of 67.82 ≈ 13.5, leading to ~81), but the authors clearly mean 20 absolute points, consistent with their earlier statement in the abstract: 'improve the best reported results of SSMs on the PathX-256 task by 20 absolute points'. The real issue arises in the next part of the sentence: the authors claim they are improving over a previously reported 63.1% by Dao et al. (2022), but the S4 baseline in their own table is at 67.82%. This suggests that either (1) Dao et al. (2022) reported 63.1% under different conditions (e.g., without the full setup), or (2) the 67.82% result they cite for S4 (Gu et al., 2022a) is already higher than Dao et al.’s number. The PAPER SUMMARY notes 'improved upon the best reported accuracy on PathX-256 by 20 absolute points (67 → 87)', which supports that their baseline is 67.82, not 63.1. The mention of Dao et al. (2022) as reporting 63.1% might be accurate if they used extra data or a different variant. The text does not clarify that the 63.1% figure might refer to a different model or setting, and juxtaposing it with their own 20-point gain could mislead readers into thinking the total gain is even larger. The statement risks being read as claiming a 87.11 - 63.1 ≈ 24-point improvement from prior work, when in fact their baseline (67.82) is already 4.7 points above Dao et al.’s. This conflation of different baselines without clarification could exaggerate the perceived novelty. The intent is to emphasize the magnitude of the gain due to SPT, but the comparison to an outdated or weaker baseline without qualification is potentially misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguous Phrasing of Transformer vs. S4 Average Performance Comparison
comments.quote: As summarized in Table 2, SPT leads to dramatic performance gains for Transformers with performance gains ranging from 8 _−_ 15% across tasks, even surpassing the average performance of a well-tuned S4 (86 _._ 2 vs 86 _._ 1).
comments.explanation: The text claims that SPT Transformers surpass the average performance of a well-tuned S4 with '86.2 vs 86.1'. However, in Table 2, the average for 'Transformers + Rotary + Masked SPT' is 86.21 and for 'S4 (Gu et al., 2022a)' is 86.09. Thus, the Transformer average is indeed higher (86.21 > 86.09), and rounding to 86.2 vs 86.1 is reasonable. The issue is that the phrase 'well-tuned S4' refers to the from-scratch S4 model, but the S4 + Masked SPT result achieves 86.75, which is higher than the SPT Transformer. So the comparison is asymmetric: the Transformer is compared to the *from-scratch* S4, not the SPT version. Given that both models undergo the same SPT treatment, the fair comparison between architectures should be between 'S4 + Masked SPT' (86.75) and 'Transformers + Rotary + Masked SPT' (86.21), in which case S4 still holds a small edge. The authors correctly state earlier that 'SPT Transformers surpass... both trained from scratch and SPT versions of S4 on 3 out of 6 tasks', so they are not fully overstating. But the phrasing 'surpassing the average performance of a well-tuned S4 (86.2 vs 86.1)' uses 'well-tuned' to describe the from-scratch S4, which might be inaccurate—'well-tuned' suggests optimal configuration, yet SPT is a critical tuning step. The comparison would be more transparent if they clarified they are comparing SPT Transformers to from-scratch S4, not to the best available S4 result. Without this, a reader might conclude that SPT eliminates the S4 advantage entirely, when in fact S4 still outperforms when both are pretrained.
